biography:
🌱 We exist to help you grow.
🌻 Our seeds: @botanical_interests

items.caption_text:
BASIL GROWING HACK - Most people let basil grow straight up, eventually wondering why it looks tall, spindly, and exhausted. However, there is a simple trick to transform your harvest by cutting just above the nodes. Look for the little crossroads on the stem where tiny leaves are waiting to sprout, because that one small snip changes the entire biological trajectory of the plant by shifting its focus from height to health.

When you prune above a node, the plant does more than just grow back. It diverts its energy into two new branches from that exact point, effectively turning one stem into two. If you repeat this process on those new stems, you suddenly have four. Through this exponential growth, you stop the plant from stretching vertically and instead force it to build a wide and sturdy foundation.

Give it a few months and the difference is night and day. Unpruned basil rushes to flower and seed as it becomes sparse and woody, while pruned basil transforms into a dense bush with massive harvests. It is the same plant and the same soil, but your decisions dictate the outcome. By guiding the growth rather than just watching it, you turn a tired herb into a lush kitchen powerhouse.

items.caption_text:
Comment “homestead” to see us build a homestead from scratch!
STARTING A HOMESTEAD - My homestead is proof that even a small piece of land can be engineered into a food-producing system that runs on intention. Raised beds keep everything organized and easy to maintain, rainwater capture turns every roof into usable irrigation, and a backyard test garden constantly filters in what actually performs. Add chickens cycling nutrients back into the soil and an in-ground section for high-yield crops, and you get a setup that quietly compounds season after season.

It’s not about having more space, it’s about making every square foot work together. Each part of the system feeds the next, turning waste into input and effort into long-term return. That’s how a third-acre ends up producing hundreds of pounds of food when every piece has a purpose.

And if you want to see that same mindset applied from the ground up, we started a brand new homestead from scratch in Maine in partnership with @homedepot. Follow along as we build it piece by piece and show exactly how a raw space turns into a working food system. #ad

items.caption_text:
BANANA LEAF POT - Most people see a banana leaf pot and think it’s just a pretty old-school trick, but it actually works like giving a seedling a house it never has to move out of. With plastic pots, it’s like packing a plant into a box and then ripping it open at the worst moment, breaking tiny root hairs that are doing all the real work. With banana leaves, you just plant the whole thing straight into the soil and the roots keep growing like nothing ever changed.

What’s really happening is the leaf slowly turns into soil, like the pot is dissolving back into the garden and becoming part of it. It feeds the microbes that feed your plant, like turning the container into a slow meal right at the roots. And because it breathes instead of trapping heat and water like plastic, it acts more like a woven basket than a sealed cup, letting air move through so the roots stay healthy instead of suffocating. Nature’s smartest designs are usually the simplest materials.

items.caption_text:
CHICKEN COOP HACK - Most people think cleaning more = healthier coop, but the deep litter method flips that idea. Instead of removing waste, you’re building a living system. Each layer of manure + bedding becomes fuel for microbes. Think of it like a forest floor… nothing gets “cleaned,” yet nothing smells. That’s because beneficial microbes are actively breaking waste down in real time.

The real shift is thinking in ratios, not mess. Chicken manure is nitrogen-heavy, wet, and quick to release ammonia. Add a carbon-rich material like @grubblyfarms hemp bedding, and suddenly you’ve got balance. The bedding acts like a sponge holding moisture while also feeding microbes with carbon. When that balance is right, microbes outcompete odor-causing processes and transform raw waste into stable organic matter.

Maintenance is simple but strategic. Every few weeks, add a fresh layer to keep that carbon-to-nitrogen ratio in check. If you smell ammonia, that’s your signal the system is out of balance add more dry bedding and improve airflow. Over time, the coop becomes self-sanitizing as microbial populations explode. After about six months, you’ve got partially composted material ready to finish in a pile and eventually feed your garden soil. #ad

items.caption_text:
DRAGON FRUIT PRUNING - When your plant turns into a jungle, more growth isn’t always better. Every stem can flower, but the plant can’t support an unlimited number of fruits at once. It’s like a factory with too many orders and not enough workers. You end up with smaller fruit, slower ripening, and stressed plants instead of a clean, productive harvest.

So what’s actually happening when you make these cuts? Dragon fruit allocates energy through its stems, and overcrowding spreads those resources thin. By thinning stems so they don’t touch, you improve light exposure and airflow, which boosts photosynthesis and reduces disease pressure. Upright, top-heavy growth is also structurally weak, like stacking weight on a narrow tower, so removing those stems prevents breakage and redirects energy into stronger, fruiting arms.

And nothing goes to waste. Those cuttings are basically clones, ready to root and grow into new plants, often worth around $12 each. Pruning isn’t just cleanup, it’s resource management. You’re telling the plant where to invest its energy, turning wild growth into focused production and stronger, more reliable harvests.

items.caption_text:
FAST FOOD FERTILIZER - What happens if you “fertilize” tomatoes with Taco Bell? Three identical plants, three Cheesy Gordita Crunches buried three different ways: whole, chopped, and minced. The idea was simple… more surface area = faster breakdown = more available nutrients. Kind of like turning a log into wood chips vs leaving it whole. Same material, totally different speed of decomposition.

Here’s where the science kicks in. Plants don’t eat food scraps directly, they rely on microbes to break organic matter down into forms roots can absorb. When you bury something dense and greasy, you’re basically asking soil microbes to work overtime. The minced taco had more surface area, so microbes could access it faster, while the whole taco decomposed slowly, tying up nitrogen in the process. That early “boost” you see in growth? Often just a temporary flush, not sustained nutrition.

The result? The control plant, with nothing buried underneath, pulled 15 pounds of tomatoes, nearly double the taco-fed plants. Why? Because nutrients only count when they’re fully broken down and bioavailable. Until then, they can actually compete with your plant for resources. Lesson here: compost first, feed later. Your tomatoes don’t want fast food… they want finished soil.

items.caption_text:
LOGS IN RAISED BED - Two identical raised beds, one big question: does Hugelkultur actually outperform a standard soil-filled bed? @jacquesinthegarden set up a clean test with the same soil and setup, but one bed swaps the bottom third for logs and wood chips, hauled in with @gorillamadeproducts’ Gorilla Cart. Think of it like two houses with the same paint job, but completely different foundations underneath.

Here’s the science: as that buried wood breaks down, microbes and fungi move in and build underground networks that cycle nutrients and improve soil structure. It acts like a slow-release sponge or even a buried pantry, holding water and feeding plants over time. That’s why Hugelkultur beds often stay moist longer and can grow more resilient plants, plus you save about 30 percent on soil upfront.

But nothing in nature is static. As the wood decomposes, it shrinks like a settling mattress, and the whole bed sinks with it. That means you’ll keep adding soil to top it off, trading early savings for long-term maintenance. Jacques is watching it closely, but the big takeaway is this: you’re not just building a bed, you’re setting up a living system that changes over time.

items.caption_text:
Comment “FIG” and I'll send you the link to get your very own
PRUNING FIG TREES - Pruning isn’t just about making things look tidy, you’re actually telling the plant exactly where to put its energy. Think of your tree like a factory with a limited power supply. Figs only grow on new wood, so last year’s branches are basically retired workers. When you cut the tree back, you flip a switch that triggers a flush of fresh growth. That new wood is exactly where your fruit will form. If you don’t prune, you will end up with a giant bush of leaves but very little fruit to show for it.

Timing matters just as much as the cuts. Pruning when the tree is dormant and the leaves have dropped works with the plant’s natural rhythm. The tree has all its energy tucked away in the roots for winter. When spring hits, it pushes that power into the remaining buds. By cutting branches back hard, you are concentrating all that stored energy into just a few spots. This leads to much stronger shoots and way more fruit.

As you shape the tree, you want to clear out any awkward or crossing branches to keep the air moving. Taking out about 30 percent of the tree might feel scary, but it creates a perfect balance. The best part is that those cuttings aren’t trash. You can root them to start brand new fig trees for free. With a few smart cuts, you aren’t just boosting this season’s harvest, you are setting up future ones too.

items.caption_text:
GROWING TOMATO IN CONTAINERS - Planting tomatoes in containers isn’t just about saving space. It’s about being the master of your plant’s universe. Think of a 15 gallon pot like a big, open floor plan for a house. It gives the roots plenty of room to stretch out and find food and water. In a small pot, the roots hit the walls too fast and feel cramped, which tells the plant to slow down growth. Pair that space with a quality mix like @foxfarmsoilandfertilizer and you’re setting up a root zone that’s built to perform from day one.

When it’s time to move your plant into its permanent home, you want a hole that fits like a glove. This keeps the roots happy and prevents stress. Dropping some fertilizer right into that hole is like giving your plant a packed lunch exactly where it needs it, helping it settle in fast. I also like to trim off those bottom leaves so the plant focuses its energy on upward growth instead of wasting it near the soil surface.

Once the plant is in, you have to keep the environment stable. Mulch acts like a protective layer to keep the soil cool and hold onto moisture. You want to avoid rollercoaster watering where the soil swings from dry to soaked, because that is how you end up with cracked fruit or blossom end rot. Every deep watering moves nutrients through the soil, so staying consistent with a liquid feed keeps growth steady and strong.

items.caption_text:
GARLIC GROWING EXPERIMENT - Every garlic clove is essentially a stored energy package, and how that energy is used determines the quality of the bulb you harvest. When garlic is planted too deep, such as 6 inches, it exhausts its energy simply trying to reach the surface. This leaves less fuel available for bulb development, which results in smaller yields and a more difficult harvest.

Clove size plays a critical role in this process. Larger cloves are packed with more stored carbohydrates, which fuel stronger early growth and larger leaves. This leads to bigger bulbs, creating a direct correlation between input and output. Interestingly, planting a whole garlic head revealed that while multiple cloves growing together will compete for resources, they can still produce a cluster of solid bulbs in a single spot.

The largest bulbs resulted from giving the garlic the best possible start by soaking cloves in a mixture of fertilizer and hydrogen peroxide before planting them at a standard depth. The peroxide helps reduce disease pressure and increases oxygen levels around the clove, while the nutrients kickstart root development. When a plant establishes strong roots early, it can focus its energy where it matters most: growing large, healthy bulbs underground.

items.caption_text:
TRANSPLANTING MADE EASY - Transplanting into a bigger container is kind of like moving a kid out of a cramped room into a space where they can finally stretch out. With @foxfarmsoilandfertilizer, the official soil of Epic Gardening, you’re not just giving plants more room, you’re setting them up with a rich environment to actually grow into. When roots stay too long in a small pot, they start circling like they’re stuck in a loop, which makes it harder for them to take in water and nutrients.

A simple trick makes that move a lot smoother. Before you take the plant out, press the small pot into the new soil to create a perfect mold. Think of it like making a custom seat that fits exactly right. When the hole matches the root ball, there are no awkward gaps where air can dry things out, and the plant settles in at just the right depth without wobbling or sinking.

When you pop the plant out, gently loosen the roots if they’re all tangled up. It’s like helping them find a new direction so they don’t keep going in circles. Set it into its new home, water it in, and you’ve just made the transition easier on the plant. A small step in prep but it sets the plant up to take off instead of stall. 🌱

items.caption_text:
Comment 'UP' to get your hands on zucchini seeds!
GROWING ZUCCHINI VERTICALLY - With a single stake and a bit of twine, you can train your zucchini to grow upward instead of sprawling across the soil. As the plant grows, tie it up every few inches to keep it supported and heading skyward, turning what’s usually a space-hog into a compact, manageable producer. 

The real advantage comes down to airflow and light. When zucchini leaves are lifted off the ground and spaced out vertically, moisture dries faster and air circulates more freely around the plant. That makes it much harder for fungal issues like powdery mildew to take hold. At the same time, better light exposure improves photosynthesis, helping the plant put out stronger growth and more consistent fruit. 

As it climbs, removing the lower leaves helps even more. You reduce the risk of soil-borne disease while redirecting energy toward fruit production instead of excess foliage. The result is a healthier plant, easier harvesting, and no more lost zucchinis hiding under a jungle of leaves. Chaos solved.

items.caption_text: GARDEN WATERING MISTAKES - I fixed 7 watering habits that were holding my garden back. The results? Healthier, happier plants in no time. Swipe through for the full list! 💦🌱

raw.caption: GARDEN WATERING MISTAKES - I fixed 7 watering habits that were holding my garden back. The results? Healthier, happier plants in no time. Swipe through for the full list! 💦🌱

items.caption_text:
CARROT GERMINATION EXPERIMENT - I tested every carrot germination trick I could find… and the “boring” method won.

Standard sowing: 19/20. This one just works. The seeds had consistent moisture, direct soil contact, and enough airflow. No interference and no extra steps, just the conditions carrots actually need.

Radish companion: 15/20. Pretty solid. The radishes helped break up the soil as they grew and acted as a natural row marker, which made it easier to keep watering consistent without overdoing it.

Overnight soak: 15/20. Soaking helped kickstart germination by hydrating the seed faster, but once planted, some likely struggled from reduced oxygen if the soil stayed too wet.

Pre-sprouting: 11/20. This sounds smart, but carrots really don’t like root disturbance. That first tiny taproot forms quickly, and moving it can damage it or slow it down.

Cardboard cover: 10/20. It held moisture in, which is helpful, but it also limited airflow. Seeds don’t just need water, they need oxygen too, especially early on.

Cornstarch gel: 2/20. Way too much moisture sitting around the seed. It basically created the perfect environment for mold, and most of the seeds rotted before they could sprout.

Seed tape: 0/20. Same issue, just worse. The material held onto moisture for too long, cutting off airflow and leading to complete rot.

Now the methods that failed all had one thing in common: excess moisture without airflow. So the takeaway is simple. Carrot seeds don’t need complicated tricks. They need three things: steady moisture, good soil contact, and enough oxygen to breathe.

items.caption_text:
PEST PROOFING RAISED BEDS - Protecting a raised bed works best when you think in layers, because each type of pest behaves differently. Burrowing pests move through soil, weeds rely on light to sprout, and flying insects look for direct access to leaves. So instead of one fix, you stack barriers that interrupt each of those behaviors and close off entry points from below and above.

Layer 1 is landscape fabric, placed at the very bottom. This works by blocking sunlight, which weed seeds need to germinate, keeping them dormant underneath your bed. Layer 2 is 1/2 inch hardware cloth, which acts as a physical barrier that burrowing pests like gophers, voles, and moles cannot squeeze through, while still allowing water and roots to pass. Layer 3 is cardboard, which adds a temporary barrier that blocks light and space, then slowly breaks down into organic matter, feeding soil microbes and improving structure over time.

On top, the frame and frost cloth protect from above. The frame keeps the cover stable and sealed, while the frost cloth acts like a filter, letting sunlight, air, and water through but blocking flying pests from reaching your plants. When all layers work together, you get a system that uses simple physics and biology to protect your crops, without relying on constant intervention.

raw.caption:
PEST PROOFING RAISED BEDS - Protecting a raised bed works best when you think in layers, because each type of pest behaves differently. Burrowing pests move through soil, weeds rely on light to sprout, and flying insects look for direct access to leaves. So instead of one fix, you stack barriers that interrupt each of those behaviors and close off entry points from below and above.

Layer 1 is landscape fabric, placed at the very bottom. This works by blocking sunlight, which weed seeds need to germinate, keeping them dormant underneath your bed. Layer 2 is 1/2 inch hardware cloth, which acts as a physical barrier that burrowing pests like gophers, voles, and moles cannot squeeze through, while still allowing water and roots to pass. Layer 3 is cardboard, which adds a temporary barrier that blocks light and space, then slowly breaks down into organic matter, feeding soil microbes and improving structure over time.

On top, the frame and frost cloth protect from above. The frame keeps the cover stable and sealed, while the frost cloth acts like a filter, letting sunlight, air, and water through but blocking flying pests from reaching your plants. When all layers work together, you get a system that uses simple physics and biology to protect your crops, without relying on constant intervention.

items.caption_text:
Reply with “peppers” in the comments and I will send you where I get my seeds 🌶️
TOPPING PEPPERS — Topping or pruning the main stem of a pepper plant does more than just shape it. It actually changes how the plant grows. When you remove the top of the main stem, the plant redirects energy from vertical growth into lateral branches, giving it more sites for flowers and eventually fruit.

At first, this can make the plant look weaker or slower. In my experiment, the unpruned plant grew taller and set fruit earlier because it focused all its energy on growing up. The topped plant seemed delayed at first, but by branching out from the base and middle nodes, it eventually created far more flowering sites.

After about four months, the results were clear. The topped jalapeño produced 37 peppers, nearly three times more than the unpruned plant’s 15. This shows how a simple early prune can trade quick vertical growth for a bigger, more productive harvest.

⚠️ Caveat: Don’t prune if you have a short growing season, as topping slows the plant’s development too much. For large varieties like bell peppers, topping is not recommended because the yield increase isn’t significant enough to be worth the effort.

items.caption_text:
Comment “CHAMOMILE” and I’ll let you in on my seed source 🌱
CHAMOMILE HARVEST - Chamomile is kind of like that low maintenance friend who still shows up every time. Once it settles in, it drops its own seeds and comes back on its own, so you are not restarting from scratch each season. It is an easy rhythm to work with, especially if you want something dependable that fits into almost any garden space.

One of my favorite parts is harvest time. Instead of picking flower by flower, I use a simple chamomile booper to gently scoop blooms in batches, making the process faster while keeping the plant productive. The scent is always noticeable too, light and sweet, almost like green apple candy. That aroma comes from natural compounds the plant produces, which not only make it enjoyable for us but also help attract beneficial insects into the garden.

Chamomile does more than just look nice or smell good. Those small flowers act like landing spots for pollinators, bringing more activity into your space and supporting nearby plants. Once harvested, the blooms can be dried and used for tea, preserving those same natural compounds. It is the kind of plant that gives back at every stage, easy to grow, helpful to the garden, and something you can enjoy long after harvest.

items.caption_text:
GROWING POTATOES IN BUCKETS — Did you know you can influence how productive your potato plant will be before it even hits the soil? Prep your seed potatoes by “chitting” them and leaving only two eyes to sprout. It might feel counterintuitive, but this is all about energy allocation. Instead of spreading resources across a bunch of weak shoots, the plant channels its stored starches into two strong sprouts that can support better tuber development later on.

For the soil, keep it light and rich with a 50 50 mix of peat moss and compost. This balance gives you both moisture retention and airflow, which is key because potatoes form underground stems called stolons that need loose soil to expand. As you build your bucket, think in layers. Fill a seven gallon bucket one third at a time, placing two seed potatoes per layer. This mimics hilling by encouraging more sites along the buried stems where new potatoes can form.

At each layer, mix in a bit of blood meal, fish meal, and an all purpose fertilizer to supply steady nutrients as the plant grows. Once filled, top it with straw to lock in moisture and regulate temperature, then water it in well. When the plants flower and eventually die back, that is your signal that tuber formation is complete. From just a few seed potatoes, you can end up with a full bucket harvest of 15 to 20 potatoes, all from a setup that fits in a small space.

A small bucket, a few seed potatoes, and a little strategy can go a long way 🥔🌱

items.caption_text:
Comment “tomato” to get our hand-picked seed varieties perfect for beginner growers 🌱

WHEN TO PICK TOMATOES - We all love the idea of a true “vine-ripened” tomato, slowly turning red under the sun until it’s perfectly ready to pick. But here’s where the plant science gets interesting. There’s a point called the breaker stage, when a tomato hits about 50% of its final color, and at that moment, it’s already received everything it needs from the plant in terms of sugars, nutrients, and overall development.

From there, the ripening process is driven internally. Ethylene production kicks in, triggering the chemical changes that develop sweetness, aroma, and that classic tomato flavor. So whether it finishes ripening on the vine or on your kitchen counter, the end result is nearly identical, because the flavor compounds are already set in motion.

Harvesting at the breaker stage just means you’re working with the plant instead of against it. You reduce the risk of pests, splitting after heavy rain, or losing fruit right before it peaks. Turns out, the smartest “vine-ripened” tomato might just be the one you bring in a little early 🍅

items.caption_text:
Grow these edible beauties by commenting “nasty” below!
GROWING NASTURTIUMS - Nasturtiums are one of those plants that quietly earn their place in the garden. Every part of the plant is edible including the leaves, flowers, stems, and seeds, all with a naturally peppery flavor that comes from mustard oils, the same compounds found in crops like arugula and radishes. Toss the flowers into salads, use the leaves for a little bite, or pickle the seeds for a surprisingly good substitute for capers.

They are also incredibly easy to grow. Nasturtiums actually thrive in poorer soils because excess nitrogen pushes leafy growth instead of flowers, which is why they often perform better where other crops struggle. Plant them once and they frequently reseed themselves, meaning a single planting can quietly return year after year with almost no effort.

Where they really shine is along the edges of a raised bed. Their bright flowers and chemical signals tend to attract pests like aphids, pulling pressure away from your vegetables and acting as a natural trap crop. Plant them on the borders and you are not just adding color, you are creating a living buffer that helps protect your harvest.

And the best part? A plant that feeds the soil, feeds the pollinators, feeds you… and still looks good doing it. Not a bad deal for something that started as a simple border flower.

items.caption_text:
Comment “CARROT” to check out our full carrot seed collection!

CARROT GERMINATION TRICK - Carrot seeds have one major weakness during germination: they dry out fast. Because the seeds are so small, they’re usually planted very shallow, which means the top layer of soil can lose moisture quickly to sun and wind. Pressing a stick into loose soil to create a shallow trench helps concentrate moisture in that small channel and improves seed-to-soil contact, both of which are key signals that trigger germination.

Watering the trench before sowing is another small step that makes a big difference. When the soil is already evenly moist, the seed coat can begin absorbing water immediately. This process, called imbibition, activates the enzymes inside the seed that kickstart germination. If dry soil is watered after sowing, the water can sometimes shift the tiny seeds around or leave them unevenly hydrated.

Placing a stick directly over the row acts like a simple micro-moisture barrier. It lightly shades the soil surface and slows evaporation right where the seeds are sitting. Because carrot seeds are planted so shallow, even a short dry spell can interrupt germination. Keeping that top layer consistently moist gives the seeds the stable conditions they need to sprout. Once the first seedlings begin to emerge, remove the stick and let them grow freely. A small adjustment like this can be the difference between scattered sprouts and a clean, evenly filled row of carrots. 🥕

items.caption_text:
What is “WORKABLE SOIL”? Shannie from @botanical_interests is here to break it down - After winter, garden soil usually isn’t ready to plant in right away. Freezing temperatures and snowmelt often leave the soil compacted and waterlogged, which creates that sticky, muddy texture gardeners dread. What we’re really waiting for is soil that’s “workable” or “friable,” meaning it has dried enough to become loose, crumbly, and easy for roots to grow through.

A quick way to check is with a simple soil test. Dig about 6 to 8 inches down, grab a handful of soil, and squeeze it into a ball. Then toss that ball about 6 inches into the air and catch it in your palm. It might feel a little silly, but this test gives you a fast read on whether your soil is ready to work.

Here’s how to interpret the results. If the ball stays completely intact, the soil is still too wet and needs more time. If it breaks into two or three chunks, you’re getting close, but it likely needs a few more days to dry. But if the ball crumbles apart when it lands, that’s the sweet spot. Your soil is friable and ready for planting. 🌱

items.caption_text:
Want this amazing tree for your garden? Comment LOQUAT and I’ll send you the link to grab one 🌳

LOQUAT TREE BENEFITS - Loquat is one of those fruit trees that doesn’t get talked about enough. It’s incredibly low-maintenance, grows well in many climates, and once it’s established, it can produce a surprising amount of fruit each season. If you’re looking for a productive tree that doesn’t demand much attention, this one is worth considering.

One thing I always recommend is starting with a grafted loquat tree instead of growing from seed. Seed-grown trees often produce fruit that’s inconsistent or not very flavorful. A grafted variety gives you a much better shot at those sweet-tangy loquats people love. Just give the tree plenty of sunlight and plant it in well-draining soil, because loquats really don’t like having “wet feet.”

Another interesting side of this plant is how useful it’s been beyond the garden. Loquat leaves have been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries, often brewed into tea to support digestion and respiratory health. Between the fruit for jams and fresh eating and the leaves for tea, it’s one of those trees that almost feels like a small living pharmacy in the backyard.

items.caption_text:
GROWING WASABI - Wasabi might be one of the most challenging plants I’ve ever tried to grow. I’ll be honest, I’ve already failed twice. This third attempt is me hoping I finally get it right. It’s a notoriously tricky crop, and there’s a reason most people never try growing it at home.

In the wild, wasabi grows along cold, misty mountain streams where the temperature stays cool and the air stays humid. Trying to recreate that environment in the garden is tough. On top of that, the plant itself is surprisingly fragile. The stems and leaves snap easily, so even handling it takes a little extra care.

And patience is a huge part of the process. Wasabi can take up to three years before the rhizome is ready to harvest. When you think about how difficult it is to grow and how long it takes, it makes sense why real, fresh wasabi can sell for over $250 per kilogram. If I can keep this one alive, it’ll definitely be worth the wait.

Fun fact: the spicy kick of real wasabi comes from compounds called isothiocyanates, which activate receptors in your nose rather than your tongue. That’s why the heat feels sharp but fades quickly compared to chili peppers!
